target.name: Q-Factor
target.founder: Nir Davidson, Ofer Firstenberg, Yoav Sagi, Guy Raz

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $24M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Quantum Computing
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Quantum Computing
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Quantum Computing

data.narrative:
Q-Factor is a seed-stage quantum computing company that has raised $24M to date. The quantum computing sector comprises 11 comparable entities: 3 public companies (IonQ, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum) and 8 private firms including IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion.

The valuation analysis relies on stage-progression methodology, which applies a 2.5x step-up multiplier typical of seed-to-next-stage advancement. Using Q-Factor's $24M total raised as a valuation proxy, this method implies a post-money valuation of $60M. The peer-median approach could not be applied due to insufficient comparable datapoints at the same stage and valuation tier within quantum computing; while 7 sector peers exist, none met the threshold of at least 3 similarly positioned companies necessary for reliable median comparison.

The triangulated valuation range is $51M to $69M, with a median of $60M. This range reflects the stage-progression result and conservative downside/upside scenarios around it. The primary uncertainty is the absence of narrow peer benchmarking; reliance on a single methodological pillar limits confidence in pinpointing valuation precisely within the range. Additional comparable financials from adjacent-stage quantum computing companies would strengthen the analysis.
